topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly aimed stone. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming beneath my ribs. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously researched piece on urban decay, the one I’d poured months into, was attributed to a stranger. A stranger whose name was slick and familiar. I reread the text, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. It felt like a physical violation. I didn't respond immediately. Instead, I spent an hour just staring at the screen, my fingers drumming an uneven rhythm on the desk. Every instinct screamed at me to fire off an angry rebuttal, to demand an explanation, but a strange, stubborn inertia held me captive. I just kept scrolling, the frustration simmering just beneath the surface, refusing to let the feeling boil over into something messy.

[story 2]

When the notification popped up, a cold wash swept over me. I felt my shoulders immediately tense, pulling up toward my ears. It was a sharp, unpleasant jolt. I clicked on the article, and the familiar words swam before my eyes, yet they belonged to someone else. A deep, slow burn started in my chest. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and took a long, deliberate sip of cold coffee. I wanted to smash the keyboard, to scream into the void, but I forced myself to breathe. I reviewed the evidence, cataloging the discrepancies. My voice, when I finally typed the initial draft of my response, was clipped and unnervingly level. There was a stubborn refusal in the tone, a quiet insistence that this wrongness would not be ignored, even if I had to fight through the sheer exhaustion of trying to make it happen.

[story 3]

The confirmation arrived, stark white against the dark background of my monitor. A slow, heavy sigh escaped me, a sound more of resignation than defeat. I picked up the printout, the paper feeling flimsy and insulting in my hands. My knuckles turned white as I gripped the edges. The injustice of it settled deep in my core, a heavy, immovable weight. I paced the small office, the sound of my footsteps echoing hollowly. I walked to the window, watching the city lights blur, feeling utterly immovable. I didn't call anyone. I didn't draft a scathing letter. Instead, I sat down, stared at the document, and began meticulously outlining a counter-narrative, a precise, cold dissection of the situation. It was a slow, grinding process, fueled by a deep, unyielding resolve to prove the truth, regardless of the immediate setback.
